back: A self-governed or sovereign kingdom; the independent state established by Shivaji Maharaj.
The formal title of the Maratha sovereign, meaning 'protector of the people'.
Dnyaneshwar, Namdev, Tukaram, and Ramdas; they provided the cultural foundation for the Maratha rise.
A tactic using speed, surprise, and terrain knowledge to defeat larger armies.
The strong mountain fortress where Shivaji Maharaj was crowned in 1674.
Shivaji's conquest of the South (Tamil Nadu and Karnataka), providing strategic depth.
The Council of Eight Ministers established by Shivaji for efficient administration.
The Prime Minister in the Maratha administration.
The Finance Minister in the Ashta Pradhan Council.
A tax of 25% of revenue levied from non-Maratha provinces for protection.
An additional 10% tax levied on top of Chauth from non-Maratha regions.
Cavalry soldiers whose horses and equipment were provided by the state.
Cavalry soldiers who provided their own horses and equipment.
A cursive form of Devanagari used for rapid Maratha correspondence.
A 'tiger's claw' weapon used by Shivaji for close-range combat (e.g., against Afzal Khan).
A dictionary commissioned by Shivaji to promote Sanskrit/Marathi administrative vocabulary.
The Maratha Queen who was the architect of the northward expansion in the early 18th century.
The ruler of Indore famous for welfare, temple restoration (Kashi Vishwanath), and the Maheshwar weaving industry.
The Maratha naval leader who successfully challenged European maritime supremacy.
The scholar-king of Thanjavur who patronized medicine, printing, and classical arts.
A disastrous Maratha defeat against the Afghans, followed by a rapid recovery under the Peshwas.
The 'Royal Edict' by Ramachandrapant Amatya defining forts as the 'core of the state'.
A traditional scepter signifying sovereignty and fair rule (associated with the opening of the chapter).
